metadata.summary: Map stakeholders by power and interest, establish decision rights, and run systematic engagement. PM brokers cross-functional alignment using a register with explicit comms directions.

content.title: Stakeholder Management
content.summary: Stakeholder management is identifying everyone affected by your product, mapping them by power and interest, and running a systematic engagement cadence. The PM acts as broker — translating between engineering, design, sales, support, and executives — not as a communication bottleneck. The register must encode who makes decisions, not just who gets updates.

why.p: Products fail due to stakeholder misalignment, not technology. Silent stakeholders who block late, executives who are "surprised," and conflicting requirements all share one root cause: no systematic map of who matters, what they fear, and how decisions get made. A register with decision_rights and comms_direction columns converts political ambiguity into a routable contract.

list.item: PM inherits a product line and needs to know who to talk to before the first 1:1.
An executive flags being "surprised" — fix is an explicit upward-comms cadence, not more updates.
A feature crosses 3+ functional silos — the register encodes the brokerage rules.
Two stakeholders disagree publicly — use power × interest × attitude to choose the right forum.
Pre-launch GTM coordination: marketing, sales, support, legal must each sign a Ready gate.
The "stakeholder" is actually a customer — use customer-discovery / Jobs-to-be-Done instead.
Engineering-only refactor with no business stakeholders — RFC + tech-lead approval suffices.
The conflict is a strategy disagreement — no comms cadence can paper over a missing strategy; run a strategy session first.
Solo founder — you are all roles; skip.
Org dysfunction (dual-hatted execs, unclear decision rights) — the register exposes it but cannot fix it.

section.name: Stakeholder Types
section.p: Six categories: End Users (customers/employees, need product value); Decision Makers (executives/sponsors, need business outcomes); Influencers (advisors/thought leaders, need credibility and early access); Builders (developers/designers, need clear requirements); Support (customer success/sales, need product knowledge); External (partners/regulators, need compliance and compatibility).

rule.statement: Map every stakeholder to one of four cells: High Power/High Interest (manage closely), High Power/Low Interest (keep satisfied), Low Power/High Interest (keep informed), Low Power/Low Interest (monitor only).
rule.rationale: The grid converts the implicit political map into a routable communication plan; without it, PMs spend equal time on every stakeholder regardless of impact.

rule.statement: Measure power by decisions blocked or unblocked in the last quarter, not by org-chart title.
rule.rationale: A Principal Engineer or Distinguished Architect often has more product veto power than a director; title-based power maps miss these nodes.

section.name: Engagement Levels
section.p: Four levels: Partner (active co-creation, regular meetings); Involved (regular input, reviews, feedback sessions); Informed (one-way updates, newsletters, demos); Monitor (minimal contact, periodic check-ins). Assign based on power x interest, not by seniority.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Identify: brainstorm using six questions: who uses, pays, approves, builds, supports, and could block this product?
rule.rationale: Silent blockers — those who could block later but are not yet engaged — are the most dangerous category; the six questions force them into view early.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Analyze each stakeholder: what do they want, what do they fear, power level, interest level, and current attitude (supporter/neutral/resistor)?
rule.rationale: Attitude classification determines the engagement tactic; a Resistor with high power needs a 1:1 strategy meeting, not a newsletter.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Plan engagement with explicit cadence, format, content, and named owner for each stakeholder.
rule.rationale: Vague engagement plans ("communicate regularly") decay in one sprint; an explicit cadence ("biweekly 1:1 on Wednesday") survives PM vacation.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Execute and adapt: track satisfaction, refresh attitudes monthly for executives (not quarterly), and handle conflicts proactively using the forum appropriate to the power x attitude cell.
rule.rationale: Executive attitudes turn faster than feature-team attitudes; a VP who was Supporter at kickoff can be Neutral by Q2 if metrics dip and no contact was maintained.

rule.statement: Add comms_direction (upward/lateral/downward/external) and decision_rights (Approve/Consult/Inform/None) columns to every stakeholder register.
rule.rationale: Without these columns, every stakeholder appears to have veto power; with them, the PM can confidently say "you are Consult on this, not Approve" — and back it up.

rule.statement: Include the management chain (manager, skip-level) as first-class stakeholders.
rule.rationale: PMs who omit skip-levels from the register are blindsided by skip-level reviews; the management chain is not a background stakeholder.

antipattern.description: PM centralizes all cross-functional communication through themselves (PM as bottleneck).
antipattern.reason: The register should route, not funnel — some engineering-to-sales communication should happen directly with the PM informed, not as a relay. Single-point-of-failure PMs create delays and knowledge silos.

antipattern.description: PM escalates every decision upward, training executives to ignore communications and the team to bypass the PM.
antipattern.reason: Define explicitly what does NOT go upward as deliberately as what does; keep a written "I will decide" column on the decision log.

antipattern.description: Lateral capture: a friendly head-of-sales becomes the loudest voice and silently steers the roadmap while appearing as one row among many in the register.
antipattern.reason: Track time spent per stakeholder monthly; if it does not match power x interest, recalibrate. The grid becomes inaccurate when attention diverges from the model.

antipattern.description: Same communication format and content sent to all stakeholders regardless of role.
antipattern.reason: Executives need outcomes and risk; engineers need decisions and constraints; customers need benefits and how-to. One message serves none of them well.

antipattern.description: Ignoring silent stakeholders — not proactively seeking input from those who don't speak up in meetings.
antipattern.reason: Silent stakeholders who were not engaged become the loudest blockers at launch; they had concerns they never voiced because nobody asked.

antipattern.description: Only sharing positive updates; hiding challenges until they are crises.
antipattern.reason: Executive trust is built by surfacing bad news early, not by delivering only good news — which trains executives to assume the PM is hiding problems.

antipattern.description: No escalation path defined — no explicit process for resolving conflicts within a time limit.
antipattern.reason: Without an escalation path, conflicts fester; an explicit "unresolved in 48h escalates to executive sponsor" creates the pressure needed to resolve things before they block delivery.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: The PM-flavored stakeholder pipeline is three loops, with the PM (or a PM-shaped subagent) as the broker node, never as the data terminator. (1) Translation loop — every cross-functional artifact (PRD, status update, incident note) must exist in three audience-tailored forms: exec brief (outcomes + risk), eng spec (decisions + constraints), GTM brief (positioning + readiness). The agent generates all three from one source-of-truth doc and the register chooses the route. (2) Decision-rights loop — before any meeting, the PM agent reads the register, identifies the named decision-maker (RACI A) for the topic, and refuses to convene unless that person or a delegated proxy is present; otherwise the meeting becomes status theatre. (3) Upward-comms cadence loop — the agent drafts the executive brief on a fixed cadence (e.g. first Monday of each month) using a template that forces three sections: what changed since last brief, the one decision I need from you, the one risk I am tracking. Anything more is noise; an exec's attention budget is the constraint, not yours.

section.name: Recommended Subagents
section.p: faion-pm-agent (referenced in this methodology's frontmatter; spec at .../product-manager/agents/) — runs discovery, drafts the PM-flavored register including a decision-rights column and a comms-direction column (upward / lateral / downward / external).

faion-sdd-executor-agent — for any SDD task touching multiple stakeholders, the executor reads the register's decision-rights column and refuses to mark a task done until the named approver has logged sign-off; this prevents the classic PM failure of "eng shipped, legal had not approved".

faion-business-analyst (sibling skill) — pairs with the PM to extract requirements from each stakeholder; the BA-flavored interview transcripts are the evidence the PM agent cites when classifying interest and attitude.

faion-marketing-manager / faion-gtm-strategist — consumes the external slice (key customers, partners, analysts) and the lateral slice (sales, support, marketing) to drive launch readiness; PM owns the register, marketing/GTM owns execution against it.

password-scrubber-agent — non-negotiable before any register or upward brief lands in a public repo; PM registers contain personal phone numbers, exec emails and sometimes salary-band information embedded in role definitions.

section.name: PM Register Generation Pattern
section.p: You are a PM stakeholder agent. Read PRD at path, latest 4 weekly status notes, the org chart at path, and CODEOWNERS. Build a stakeholder register with these columns: name, role, comms_direction (upward|lateral|downward|external), decision_rights (Approve|Consult|Inform|None), power (cite a recent decision they blocked or unblocked), interest (H/M/L), attitude (Supporter|Neutral|Resistor|Unknown), cadence (specific: "biweekly 1:1 Wed", not "ad-hoc"), channel (1:1, RFC comments, exec brief, sales weekly), owner_of_relationship (PM|TPM|EM|head-of-X). Mark Unknown for any field you cannot ground in cited source. Output to .aidocs/product/stakeholder-register.md.

section.name: Upward-Comms Brief Pattern
section.p: Read .aidocs/product/stakeholder-register.md and .aidocs/product/decisions.md. For stakeholders where comms_direction=upward AND decision_rights in {Approve, Consult}, draft this month's brief. Hard constraints: 1 page maximum; 3 sections: "What changed", "One decision I need", "One risk I'm tracking"; No status table; status lives in the dashboard, link it; Plain English; no internal codenames without a one-line gloss; Surface the bad news in section 1, not section 3. Do not send. Output a draft per executive for human review.

section.name: Conflict-Mediation Pattern
section.p: Stakeholder A wants X. Stakeholder B wants Y. Read the register and the roadmap. Recommend the right forum to resolve (1:1, written RFC, steering, escalate to common manager). Cite the power × interest × attitude rationale. Draft an opening message for the chosen forum that does not pre-decide.

section.name: Best Practices
section.p: Add a comms_direction column (upward | lateral | downward | external). The PM-specific failure modes cluster by direction; you cannot diagnose "I'm losing trust upward" without it.

Add a decision_rights column (RACI-style: Approve / Consult / Inform / None). Without this, every stakeholder seems to have veto power; with it, the PM can confidently say "you are Consult on this, not Approve".
Treat the management chain as first-class stakeholders, including skip-levels. PMs who omit their manager's manager from the register are blindsided by skip-level reviews.
Run the register with the EM and TPM, not as a PM artifact. Joint ownership prevents the PM from grading their own homework on attitudes.
Keep an exec sub-register on a tighter refresh (monthly), separate from the operational register. Executive attitudes turn faster than feature-team attitudes.
For each Resistor, write down what would change their mind in one sentence and revisit monthly. If the answer is "nothing", they are not a Resistor; they are a constraint — replan.
The "one decision I need from you" line in every upward brief is the single biggest predictor of useful exec engagement. Force it.
Pre-mortem the brief: "If this exec ignores my brief, what is the most likely reason?". Usually it is too long, leads with the wrong thing, or asks for a decision that was already made.
After every major launch, run a stakeholder-retro distinct from the eng-retro: which relationships strengthened, which strained, which need explicit repair. Log decisions for the next cycle.

Tie the register to your promotion / level expectations; senior-PM / GPM rubrics reward specific evidence of executive influence and cross-functional conflict resolution. The register and decision log are that evidence.

rule.statement: Require the agent to cite a policy document, RACI matrix, or written email when populating decision_rights; lint for unsourced rows.
rule.rationale: Hallucinated decision rights are the most damaging LLM error in stakeholder management — an agent confidently asserting "Head of Legal must approve" when a delegate does causes process delays and legal confusion.

rule.statement: Force executive briefs to lead with the worst news in section one, reject drafts that bury risk in section three.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to softening bad news for high-power readers; structural rules override sycophancy where stakes are highest.

rule.statement: All escalations must go through humans — never automate an escalation that emails an executive's manager based on sentiment analysis.
rule.rationale: Auto-escalation based on inferred executive sentiment is a fireable offense; the consequence is irreversible and the input (tone-scoring) is unreliable.

antipattern.description: Tone-mismatched lateral messages: an LLM-drafted message to sales reads as eng-snobbery; the same draft to eng reads as sales-fluff.
antipattern.reason: Generate per-audience variants from a structured source, never paraphrase a single draft into multiple audiences.

antipattern.description: Calendar-scraping privacy: agents mining 1:1 transcripts to update attitude classifications have a high false-positive rate and cross legal lines in some jurisdictions (EU, CA).
antipattern.reason: Get explicit consent and surface the source quote with every classification change.

antipattern.description: Persona-shifting exec proxies: when an exec's chief of staff is the actual decision proxy, an agent that addresses the exec by name in a brief routed through the CoS comes across as oblivious.
antipattern.reason: Encode the proxy in the register and have the agent address by role, not name.

antipattern.description: Multi-agent register drift: if both faion-pm-agent and faion-business-analyst write to the register, conflicting attitude classifications result.
antipattern.reason: Designate the PM agent as sole writer; BA proposes via a queue file (e.g. .aidocs/product/register-proposals.md) the PM agent reviews.

antipattern.description: Long-context token waste: feeding the entire register into every prompt.
antipattern.reason: Filter by comms_direction or decision_rights for the task at hand. A 200-row register dumped into every brief draft costs more than the briefs save.

antipattern.description: Prompt-injection via meeting notes: hostile or careless attendees can paste content into shared notes that the agent ingests.
antipattern.reason: Treat ingested transcripts as untrusted input; never let the agent take action (send mail, change register) without human confirmation.

antipattern.description: Cross-team sentiment models are biased: tools that score Slack tone tend to over-flag direct cultures (German, Dutch, Israeli) as "Resistor".
antipattern.reason: Calibrate or disable; do not let cultural communication style be encoded as attitude.

antipattern.description: Promotion-case fabrication: letting an agent draft your promotion narrative directly from the register; LLMs invent decisions you never made.
antipattern.reason: Use the decision log + register to verify, write the narrative yourself.

section.name: CLI Tools and Services
section.p: gh (GitHub CLI): Resolve CODEOWNERS and PR-reviewer history into the downward slice of the register. slack-cli / Slack Web API: Post the three audience-flavored versions of the same update to per-audience channels. notion-cli / Notion API: Maintain the executive-shareable view of the register; PMs typically already host PRDs in Notion. clay / People-data APIs: Enrich exec stakeholders with current title and org changes (catch the silent re-org). linear-cli / Linear GraphQL: Pull project subscribers and triage labels into the lateral slice. pandoc + LaTeX: Render the monthly exec brief into a board-ready PDF. mailmerge: Send the per-exec brief from a register CSV (no marketing tooling needed). mdq / yq: Diff register between commits to detect attitude / engagement drift. ngrok + Slack slash-cmd: Quick /whoapproves <feature> lookup against the register from any Slack channel.

Services: Productboard (SaaS) — Stakeholder feedback inboxes; pairs naturally with PM register. Aha! / Aha! Roadmaps (SaaS) — Built-in stakeholder portal and idea-by-stakeholder view; heavy for solo. Notion (SaaS) — Default home for the register if PRDs live there; use a DB with select fields for attitude. Coda (SaaS) — Dynamic stakeholder dashboards with formulas; good for promotion-prep evidence. Linear (SaaS) — Lateral / downward slice (eng); weak for exec. Pendo (SaaS) — Pulls user stakeholder signals (NPS, feature usage) into the register's external slice. Gainsight PX (SaaS) — CS-side stakeholder telemetry for B2B PMs. Lattice / Workday (SaaS) — Resolve current management chain; needed for the upward-cadence routing. Quantive (formerly Gtmhub) (SaaS) — OKR ownership maps; cross-reference OKR-owners against register attitudes. Glean / Coveo (SaaS) — Index past meeting notes to ground attitude classifications in evidence. Otter.ai / Fireflies (SaaS) — Auto-transcribe 1:1s; feed transcripts to the PM agent for register updates (review for sensitive language). Slack Workflow Builder (SaaS) — Routes the three audience-flavored briefs from a single source doc. Plane / OpenProject (OSS) — Self-hosted register storage if SaaS off-limits. Beeminder / Habitica (SaaS) — Personal accountability for the PM's own cadence (this is the cadence that actually slips).

template.purpose: Register template with name, power, interest, attitude, cadence, decision_rights, comms_direction
Communication matrix and escalation path template
Script flagging mismatch between PM time-spent and stakeholder power × interest
